Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd tell myself I'd study for a few hours each day, but it rarely happened that way. I'd usually end up cramming when I remembered something was due, or when a friend from my extracurricular group mentioned an assignment. I spent about 21 hours a week supposedly studying, but a lot of that was just staring at materials without really absorbing anything because my mind was elsewhere.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. Some weeks I'd barely touch anything academic, and then suddenly I'd panic and try to catch up all at once. Deadlines were supposed to motivate me, but honestly I missed so many of them that they stopped feeling real. My extracurricular commitments often took priority because at least there I felt like I belonged and wasn't constantly failing.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I got a notification or because someone in my peer support group mentioned something about the course. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I'd missed, which was always overwhelming. There wasn't really a strong pull to get me there—it felt more like checking in on bad news than anything productive.
Q4: Not really. I tried to make it a habit early on, but it never stuck. The interface felt cold and I'd get anxious every time I saw how behind I was. The only time it felt somewhat normal was when my peer support buddy would message me and we'd go through stuff together online, but even that was sporadic.
Q5: The videos were the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched quite a bit of video content—almost 200 minutes worth—because I could pause, rewind, and actually see concepts being explained. The discussion posts were okay too because sometimes other students would explain things in ways that made more sense than the official materials.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the detailed assignment guidelines. They were walls of text and my anxiety would spike just looking at them. I'd tell myself I'd get to them later, but later never came. The practice quizzes I also avoided because failing them privately still felt like failing, and I couldn't handle more evidence of how lost I was.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see immediate results or if it connected to something I actually cared about. Visual stuff that clicked quickly felt valuable. But when something required hours of reading with no clear payoff, I'd drift away. If my peers were doing it together, that helped too—made it feel less pointless.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a trap, honestly. Like, I knew I should do them to understand better, but when I was already drowning with required stuff, optional felt like a luxury I couldn't afford. Required activities at least had some external pressure, even if I usually caved under that pressure anyway. Optional stuff just got pushed to a 'someday' that never arrived.
Q9: Yeah, definitely. I'd download lecture slides and videos when I could get access at the library or a friend's place since I don't have internet at home. Then I'd try to study from those offline, scribbling notes in my notebook. A lot of my actual learning attempts happened away from any screen, just me with printed materials or my handwritten notes.
Q10: There were many times I'd be at a café with a friend, going over concepts together using my downloaded notes, and none of that showed up anywhere. Or I'd be lying in bed at night, mentally reviewing what I'd seen in a video, trying to make sense of it. The LMS only saw me when I was online, which wasn't often given my internet situation.
Q11: All the time. I'd log in, download everything I could, and then not come back for days or weeks. I'd study from those downloaded files repeatedly—rewatching videos I'd saved, rereading slides I'd printed. The LMS probably thought I watched something once, but I might have gone through it three or four times offline.
Q12: Around midterms, everything fell apart. My anxiety was through the roof, I was juggling my extracurricular responsibilities, and I just couldn't face the course anymore. Every time I thought about logging in, I'd feel this wave of dread about how much I'd missed. It became easier to just... not engage at all.
Q13: Absolutely. The workload felt impossible, and being confused about basic concepts made everything take twice as long. I was exhausted from anxiety and from trying to keep up with other parts of my life. When I did log into the LMS, I'd often just stare at the screen, too tired to actually process anything, then log off feeling worse than before.
Q14: So many times. I'd open a video and let it play while my mind wandered completely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. I was technically 'active' but I wasn't there mentally. It was like going through the motions to feel less guilty about not studying, even though I wasn't actually learning anything.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. The anxiety made it impossible to start—I'd think about how badly I'd probably do, and that paralyzed me. It was easier to focus on my extracurricular stuff where I felt competent. By the time I'd work up the courage to try, deadlines had usually passed.
Q16: Deadlines were supposed to motivate me but they mostly just stressed me out. I'd see a deadline approaching and freeze instead of act. Sometimes I'd log in right before something was due, realize I couldn't possibly finish in time, and just give up. The LMS would show me logging in near deadlines but not submitting—that was me panicking and then surrendering.
Q17: I barely got feedback because I barely submitted anything. When I did get feedback, it was usually too late for me to do anything with it. The few times I got encouraging comments, it helped a little, but my peer support connections honestly mattered more. Hearing from another student that they struggled too made me feel less alone.
Q18: The course structure felt rigid and unforgiving. If you fell behind, there was no easy way to catch up. Everything built on everything else, so missing early stuff meant later stuff made no sense. I needed more flexibility, more chances to recover from setbacks, but the structure just kept marching forward without me.
Q19: The heavy text-based content made things harder for me as a visual learner. Also, not having reliable internet meant the course design assumed access I didn't have. What helped were the videos and the peer interaction features—those felt more human. But the overwhelming amount of sequential content with strict deadlines made engagement nearly impossible once I fell behind.
Q20: I think that's literally me. I logged in a lot, watched videos, participated in some discussions, but my assignment completion was terrible. For me, it was anxiety and avoidance—I could passively consume content but the moment I had to produce something and be judged, I froze. Activity doesn't equal productivity when fear is involved.
Q21: That wasn't my experience at all—I had low activity AND performed poorly. But I imagine some people are just efficient, or they study heavily offline like I tried to do. If someone has good internet and study skills, they might not need to be on the LMS much. The platform isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on. That's about it. They miss everything that happens offline, all the emotional context, the anxiety attacks before logging in, the peer conversations that happen outside the system. They see my low completion rate but not the hours I spent trying and failing to start assignments.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to it somehow. It's not just showing up—it's being mentally present and wanting to understand. For me, engagement happened in small moments, usually with peers, usually offline. The LMS version of 'engagement' felt hollow compared to real learning moments.
Q24: The LMS can't show my anxiety levels, my lack of internet access at home, or how much I actually tried in ways that weren't trackable. It can't show the peer support conversations that kept me from giving up entirely, or the offline studying I did with downloaded materials. It sees a disengaged student, but it doesn't see someone struggling hard against invisible barriers.
